title: Rubbing Rack
desc: The rune scholars need fresh charcoal, dry sheets, and less wind if today's bark rubbings are going to be readable.

steps.text:
Runic Grove attracts two types of visitors consistently: the reverent and the convinced. The reverent come to stand under the Great Tree and feel small on purpose. The convinced arrive with tablets, charcoal, and the sort of expression that implies today will finally be the day the runes surrender. Most leave with damp shoes, a few good rubbings, and exactly as much uncertainty as they brought. The work tables beneath the east canopy are where that uncertainty gets organized into research.

The rubbing keeper lays out a tray of bark paper and charcoal sticks. "Grind the fresh charcoal if you want the bench," she says. "Or pin the drying sheets to the rack if you prefer the line. We cannot make the runes clearer. We can at least stop the supplies from behaving stupidly."

options.label: Grind fresh charcoal sticks at the work bench
Pin the bark-paper rubbings to the drying rack

steps.text:
The best rubbing charcoal is not the darkest. It is the most even, fine enough to catch the shallow bark cuts without turning every line into a smudge. You grind the charcoal down, sift out the coarse grit, and pack the finished powder back into sticks the scholars can use without cursing the tools before they even reach the Tree. By the time you finish, the bench is dusted black and your hands look like you have been in private conversation with a chimney.

The keeper tests one of the new sticks on a spare sheet and nods when the line comes up clean, dark, and narrow. "Good. Scholars always imagine breakthroughs happen in the mind. Usually they happen because someone prepared decent materials first."

options.label: Cap the charcoal tray and leave the bench stocked

steps.text:
The bark-paper sheets are still damp from the morning rubbings and fragile enough that one impatient hand or one rude gust can turn a morning's work into decorative mulch. You carry them to the drying rack under the east canopy, pin each sheet by the corners, and stagger the line so air can move between them without lifting the edges free. The rubbed runes stare back at you in repeated spirals, bars, and cuts that remain just as unreadable on paper as they are on bark.

Once the sheets are set, the rack begins doing its quiet part of the work: preserving questions in a flatter form. The keeper walks the line and taps one pinned corner with clear approval. "Better. Mystery is expensive enough without losing it to weather."

options.label: Step away from the rack and leave the sheets drying cleanly
